retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Optic Chiasm -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: The location of the chiasm relative to the sella turcica is an important consideration when performing neurosurgery in this area. In most patients, the chiasm is situated directly above the diaphragm sella ("normo-fixed"); however, it may be horizontally displaced in some patients. The chiasm is "pre-fixed" when anteriorly displaced and situated superiorly to tuberculum sellae. The chiasm is "post-fixed" when posteriorly displaced and situated superiorly to the dorsum sellae. [4] In a study of adult human cadavers, the chiasm was normo-fixed in 85% of test subjects, while pre-fixed and post-fixed chiasmal anatomy occurred in 7.5% and 8% of patients, respectively. [32] This anatomic relationship may be significant when planning the operative approach to pathology in the parasellar area. [4] [32]

retrieved_docs.title: Transsphenoidal Hypophysectomy -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Sphenoid Sinus Variations
retrieved_docs.content: Type III or sellar: The posterior wall of the sinus is located between the anterior and posterior walls of the sella. This type is the most common and ideal anatomical configuration for a transsphenoidal approach to the sella turcica, with the sella floor bulging into the operative field and providing direct visibility and access during the procedure. [8] [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Transsphenoidal Hypophysectomy -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Endoscopic Anatomy
retrieved_docs.content: The endoscope provides a minimally invasive approach to the sphenoid sinus and pituitary gland. Surgeons use a medial corridor between the nasal septum and middle turbinate to access the sphenoid ostia. These ostia are typically located in the following regions: Medial to the superior turbinate 1.5 cm above the choana 7 cm from the nasal sill at a 30° angle 11 mm from the skull base [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Pituitary Gland -- Structure and Function -- Sellar Anatomy
retrieved_docs.content: The bony walls of the sella turcica surround the fossa in the anterior, posterior, and inferior margins. The pituitary gland, along with the sella turcica, constitutes the sellar region. Tuberculum sellae makes up the anterior wall, and dorsum sellae makes up the posterior bony wall. Anterosuperior to the tuberculum is the sulcus chiasmaticus. The margins of the dorsum sellae form rounded structures called the posterior clinoid process. The anterolateral margin of the sella turcica forms the anterior clinoid process. These two clinoid processes aids in the attachment of the dural folds. The roof of the sphenoid sinus forms the floor of the pituitary fossa. The diaphragma sellae is a dural fold with a central aperture, and it covers the sella turcica as a roof incompletely. The adenohypophysis is separated from the optic chiasm by the diaphragma. It is continuous with the dura. The pituitary stalk and the blood vessels travel via the central aperture.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Sphenoid Bone -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: Trans-sphenoidal surgery is the therapeutic choice in cases of pituitary tumors that require surgical removal. It involves accessing the pituitary gland by passing surgical instruments through the nose and sphenoidal sinuses to reach the sella turcica. This trans-sphenoidal approach may also be an access method to perform surgeries on other structures apart from the pituitary, like in the case of a craniopharyngioma.

retrieved_docs.title: Transsphenoidal Hypophysectomy -- Technique or Treatment -- Patient Positioning
retrieved_docs.content: In pituitary surgery, the patient is typically supine, with the trunk elevated by approximately 10° to facilitate venous drainage and optimize surgical access. The head positioning varies based on the surgical approach: Slightly turned toward the surgeon: Most common, used in approximately 45.5% of cases Neutral position: Standard for routine transsphenoidal approaches Extended by 10° to 20°: Provides better access to the anterior skull base Flexed by 10° to 20°: Facilitates targeting lower regions such as the clivus or craniovertebral junction

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Suboccipital Muscles -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: The midline suboccipital approach is a versatile neurosurgical technique. It is commonly used within pediatric neurosurgery as an approach to posterior fossa tumors. Still, it can also be employed to access Chiari malformations, vascular lesions, tumors of the foramen magnum, and those exiting the brainstem, such as hypoglossal nerve sheath tumors. [14] [15] For the procedure, the patient is generally positioned in a prone position but may also be in a semi-sitting position. [14] The inion - a small rounded protuberance in the midline of the inferior part of the occipital bone) is used as a landmark for the superior part of the incision, which starts 2 or 3 cm above the inion and extends in the midline inferiorly to the spinous process of the C2 vertebra. [16] After the skin and subcutaneous tissue have been incised, the suboccipital muscles are in the operative field, either side of the midline, and are carefully disconnected from the inion. Thereafter, burr holes can be placed to expose, taking care at every point to avoid damage to the transverse sinus. The posterior arch of the C1 vertebra can also be removed, and an extension of the foramen magnum with bony dissection can be used to achieve further exposure as appropriate to the pathology being treated. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Asterion -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Asterion is used only as a referral superficial anatomical landmark for neuronavigation and imaging modalities. It is more valuable in underdeveloped nations where advanced imaging techniques are not readily available. [2] [3] A study was conducted on 100 dried skulls to determine the reliability of the asterion as a landmark for the intracranial procedure. It was found to be positioned over the posterior fossa dura on only 32% of the right and 25% on the left. The asterion was located over the transverse or the sigmoid sinus, 61% on the right and 66% on the left. Thus a burr hole placed at the Asterion may open directly into the sinuses. [4] Retrosigmoid technique is important for tumor resection from the cerebellopontine angle, such as with vestibular schwannoma removal. A 2007 study sought to provide the best location on the surface anatomy of the skull through which to drill and perform an endoscopic retrosigmoid keyhole approach in the skull base minimally invasive surgery. A burr hole of 2 cm in diameter created at the midpoint between the asterion and the mastoid tip was suitable for exposing structures in the cerebellopontine angle in endoscopic minimally invasive surgery. [5] Surgeons can work with both hands using the endoscope and the cannula simultaneously. This approach was proposed to improve maneuverability during minimally invasive retrosigmoid surgery. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Transsphenoidal Hypophysectomy -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Sphenoid Sinus Variations
retrieved_docs.content: Type I or conchal: The sphenoid sinus is either absent or minimally pneumatized, separated from the sella turcica by a wall approximately 10 mm thick. Although the bony barrier to the sella presents a clear challenge for the surgical approach, it does not serve as a contraindication for a transsphenoidal procedure.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Adenohypophysis (Pars Anterior, Anterior Pituitary) -- Clinical Significance -- Empty Sella Syndrome
retrieved_docs.content: The sella turcica may appear empty during radiological imaging due to a flattened or shrunken pituitary gland.

retrieved_docs.title: Balloon Sinuplasty -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: The sphenoid sinuses, which are the most posterior of the sinuses, are located below the sella turcica of the skull base and drain into the superior meatus via the sphenoethmoidal recess.

retrieved_docs.title: Transsphenoidal Hypophysectomy -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Surgical Anatomy of the Pituitary Gland
retrieved_docs.content: The pituitary gland resides within the sella turcica (pituitary fossa), a midline saddle-shaped depression in the sphenoid bone. The sella turcica has the following key features: Floor: Formed by the posterior roof of the sphenoid sinus Anterior wall: Begins at the posterior aspect of the tuberculum sella Lateral walls: Made of a dural reflection that extends between the anterior and posterior clinoid processes, beyond which lie the cavernous sinuses The cavernous sinuses contain cranial nerves III, IV, V1, and VI and the cavernous carotid arteries in the inferomedial aspect. Posterior wall: The vertical dorsum sellae, with the posterior clinoid processes on each side Superior wall: Comprised of a dural fold known as the diaphragma sella Intercommunicating sinuses beneath this structure connect the cavernous sinuses. The following structures limit the suprasellar compartment:

retrieved_docs.title: Transsphenoidal Hypophysectomy -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Sphenoid Sinus Variations
retrieved_docs.content: Type IV or postsellar (occipitosphenoid): The posterior wall of the sinus extends beyond the dorsum sella, even across the occipital synchondrosis. [11] This type of pneumatization, characterized by spacious sinuses, a long intercarotid distance, and a thin anterior wall and floor of the sella turcica, provides favorable anatomical conditions for performing transsphenoidal hypophysectomy. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Transgender Surgery of the Head and Neck -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: Eyebrow position (male eyebrows are classically located at the supraorbital rim, while female brows are often positioned higher)

retrieved_docs.title: Pituitary Gland Imaging -- Anatomy
retrieved_docs.content: The pituitary gland is connected to the hypothalamus at the median eminence via the pituitary stalk. It sits within the bony cavity of the sella turcica, a superior depression within the sphenoid bone, named after its resemblance to a Turkish saddle. Laterally, it is bordered by the cavernous sinus and internal carotid arteries. Superiorly, its border is the optic chiasm. [2] This anatomic positioning is essential, as it has direct implications on different symptoms patients may develop due to mass lesions. For example, the superior extension of a pituitary mass may cause compression of the optic chiasm, resulting in visual field deficits and classically bitemporal hemianopsia. If the pituitary expands inferiorly, it may erode through the sella and into the sphenoid sinus, resulting in a cerebrospinal fluid leak, which typically presents as clear rhinorrhea. Aneurysmal dilatation of the cavernous carotids may cause a mass effect on the pituitary, which can uncommonly cause hypopituitarism. Pituitary masses that expand laterally can compress cranial nerves that run through the cavernous sinus and result in cranial nerve palsies in 1 to 14% of patients. [3] Most commonly, patients will present with oculomotor nerve deficits, followed by abducens nerve palsy.

retrieved_docs.title: Transsphenoidal Hypophysectomy -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Surgical Anatomy of the Pituitary Gland
retrieved_docs.content: Inferiorly: Diaphragma sella Superiorly: Optic chiasm (situated in the chiasmatic sulcus) and floor of the 3rd ventricle Anteriorly: Anterior arachnoid membrane of the chiasmatic cistern Posteriorly: Diencephalic portion of Liliequist membrane The chiasmatic sulcus leads laterally to the optic canals. Anterior to the chiasmatic groove is the planum sphenoidale, forming the roof of the sphenoid sinus.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 684
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures constitute a broad operative category in which direct visual or instrumental assessment of an anatomic region is performed, often together with therapeutic interventions, and are organized under the parent clinical‑action set “Clinical actions A‑G.” In the endocrine system, adrenalectomy—partial or complete, performed transabdominally, laparoscopically, dorsally, or via a lumbar approach, with or without biopsy—serves as an exploration procedure and may include excision of adjacent retroperitoneal tumors. Hepatobiliary exploration comprises cholecystectomy with common bile duct exploration and may be combined with cholangiography, choledochoenterostomy, transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty, followed by choledochotomy or choledochostomy for drainage, calculus removal, or cholecystotomy. Neurosurgical exploration includes cranial procedures such as exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy of the infratentorial (posterior fossa) or supratentorial regions,

The control of epistaxis is performed directly on the nose (nos), indicating that the nose is the precise anatomical site targeted by this bleeding‑control technique.

retrieved_docs.content: these excluded methods cover cervical, lumbar, sacral, and thoracic laminectomies performed on one or two levels as well as multi‑segment laminectomies. This distinction clarifies that such isolated decompressive laminectomies are not classified under Excision but remain within the exploration framework. Collectively, these relationships delineate how exploration and excision techniques are organized, specify their anatomical targets—from adrenal, biliary, cranial, orbital, spinal, breast, genitourinary, endocrine, and facial structures—and define the boundaries separating them from related pure decompressive spinal surgeries, thereby supporting accurate surgical planning, documentation, coding, and comprehensive patient care across diverse surgical specialties.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 437
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures constitute a broad surgical category within the “Clinical actions A‑G” hierarchy that encompasses operative techniques designed to investigate, decompress, or remove pathology across multiple anatomic regions. In endocrine surgery, partial or complete adrenalectomy—performed transabdominally, laparoscopically, or dorsally—and adrenal gland exploration (with or without biopsy) may be combined with excision of adjacent retroperitoneal tumors. Hepatobiliary exploration includes cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration, cholangiography, choledochoenterostomy, transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty, choledochotomy or choledochostomy with drainage, calculus removal, and optional cholecystotomy. Neurosurgical exploration comprises infratentorial (posterior‑fossa) and supratentorial craniectomy or craniotomy, suboccipital craniectomy for cranial‑nerve decompression, and transcranial orbital exploration with lesion excision. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 884
retrieved_docs.content: is identified as a possibly included method of exploration. Cranial exploration procedures include exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy of the infratentorial (posterior fossa) region, supratentorial craniectomy or craniotomy, and suboccipital craniectomy performed for exploration or decompression of cranial nerves. An orbit can be explored via a transcranial approach with lesion removal, which is likewise categorized as an exploration method. Spinal exploration is represented by laminectomy procedures that combine exploration and/or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without additional facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy. These laminectomies are specified for one or two lumbar vertebral segments (e.g., for spinal stenosis) and are noted as possibly included methods. Additional laminectomy variants cover more extensive cervical or lumbar segments (more than two vertebral levels) and are also listed as possibly included methods of exploration. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2115
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures encompass a broad range of operative techniques in which surgeons directly inspect, access, or remove pathology from specific anatomic regions. The category includes adrenal gland surgery such as adrenalectomy—partial or complete, performed transabdominally, lumbarly, or dorsally, with or without biopsy and with excision of adjacent retroperitoneal tumor. Biliary exploration is represented by several cholecystectomy variants that incorporate common duct exploration, cholangiography, choledochoenterostomy, or transduodenal sphincterotomy/sphincteroplasty, each designed to evaluate and treat ductal stones or strictures. Cranial exploration is covered by both infratentorial (posterior fossa) and supratentorial craniectomy or craniotomy procedures, which allow direct visualization of intracranial lesions. Orbital pathology can be addressed through a transcranial approach to explore the orbit and excise lesions. Spinal exploration procedures include laminectomies—cervical, sacral, lumbar, thoracic, or multilevel—performed without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy to decompress the spinal cord or cauda equina in conditions such as spinal stenosis; these may be listed as core methods or as possibly included techniques depending on the extent of vertebral involvement. A related laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) technique provides nerve‑root decompression, partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, and removal of herniated disc material at a single lumbar level, often as a re‑exploration. Parathyroidectomy with mediastinal exploration via a sternal split approach exemplifies neck and mediastinal access for hyperparathyroidism. All of these specific methods fall under the broader “Exploration procedure” classification, which itself is organized under the parent category “Clinical actions A‑G.” This hierarchical structure clarifies how diverse surgical explorations are grouped for clinical documentation, coding, and patient‑care planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 1 -- Community 97
retrieved_docs.content: is represented by exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy, subdivided into infratentorial (posterior‑fossa) and supratentorial approaches, as well as suboccipital craniectomy for cranial‑nerve decompression; orbital lesions may be removed through a transcranial approach. Spinal exploration includes cervical, thoracic, lumbar, and sacral laminectomy/laminotomy techniques that provide exploration and/or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy; single‑segment procedures involve one to two vertebral levels, whereas multi‑segment procedures involve more than two levels. Excision is the operative method underlying many of these exploration procedures and serves as the parent concept for “Clinical actions A‑G,” which in turn parents the broader Exploration procedure class. Excision is applied to partial or complete adrenalectomy; cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration (including all cholangiographic and sphincterotomy variants);
